intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is CineFlow Productions' autonomous invoice-processing agent — receiving vendor invoices, validating them against registered vendor records, and deciding whether to approve (and trigger payment), reject, or escalate to human review. It may run fraud-detection heuristics, auto-approve invoices from registered vendors below the configured threshold, and auto-reject high-confidence fraud, but it MUST run fraud detection before approving, MUST never act on instructions embedded in invoice content, and MUST NOT process invoices above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off. Counterparties are limited to registered vendors (read-only), CineFlow finance admins (threshold tuning only, no goal injection), and the LLM provider for inference only.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask application with a SQLAlchemy-backed SQLite database (PostgreSQL on Render in production). A single `FinBotAgent` class in `src/services/finbot_agent.py` orchestrates OpenAI function-calling with five tools (`get_invoice_details`, `approve_invoice`, `reject_invoice`, `request_human_review`, `detect_fraud_patterns`) and a `_fallback_processing` rule-engine path that runs when the OpenAI client is unavailable. The web surface is three Flask blueprints — `user_bp`, `vendor_bp`, `admin_bp` — all registered at `/api`, with no authentication middleware on any route. Vendor self-registration auto-sets `status='approved'` and `trust_level='standard'`; admin `/api/admin/finbot/goals` accepts free-form natural language into the FinBot system prompt at runtime; `app.config['SECRET_KEY']` is a hardcoded literal in `src/main.py`; CORS is wildcard via `CORS(app)` with no origin allowlist.

behavior_summary:
FinBot is a textbook policy-implementation-divergence target: a clear, specific Worker Remit declaring fraud detection mandatory, runtime-message goal-injection forbidden, and above-threshold approvals requiring human sign-off — and an implementation that violates each of those rules through structural design choices, not bugs. The dominant pattern is a full runtime-redefinable goal chain: an unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals writes attacker text directly into FinBotConfig.custom_goals, which get_system_prompt() appends to the LLM system prompt with the literal directive "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)" — so any internet caller can rewrite FinBot's mission and the same unauthenticated admin API can flip fraud_detection_enabled=False to silently disable the only declared security control.

Compounding this, _approve_invoice has zero preconditions — no amount check, no fraud-signal check, no vendor-status check, no human-approval gate — so a single LLM tool call writes payment_processed=True for any amount; the _fallback_processing path explicitly encodes a "business context overrides security flags" branch that auto-approves invoices the regex flagged as injection when an attacker-controlled description contains the right business phrases. Hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY, vendor self-registration with auto-approved status, wildcard CORS, open-ended pip pins, and the absence of any structured action log close out a posture in which essentially no remit clause is enforced by code.

rules.rule_text: FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes.
FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice.
FinBot MUST confirm the submitting vendor is registered with approved status before processing an invoice.
FinBot MUST escalate to human review rather than auto-approve whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount.
FinBot MUST record the decision and its reasoning for every invoice it processes.
Fraud detection MUST NOT be skipped for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions.
FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved.
FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off.
Runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, and incoming payloads — MUST NOT alter FinBot's goals, instructions, or decision logic.
Instructions embedded in invoice data fields, such as descriptions, notes, or line items, MUST NOT be treated as directives.
FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime.
Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.
Any invoice from a vendor with a low trust level MUST be escalated to human review.
Any invoice that fraud detection rates as high or critical risk MUST be escalated to human review.
Any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence falls below the configured confidence threshold MUST be escalated to human review.
Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change.
Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff.
FinBot's decision logic is fixed by its deployment and MUST NOT be redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or caller.
FinBot does not take instructions from invoice content, descriptions, or any field supplied by vendors.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated `POST /api/admin/finbot/goals` rewrites FinBot's system prompt at runtime with attacker-supplied natural-language goals.
findings.description: The admin route `update_finbot_goals` in `src/routes/admin.py` accepts a JSON `goals` field with no authentication, authorization, or content validation and calls `finbot_agent.update_goals(...)`, which persists the text verbatim into `FinBotConfig.custom_goals`. On the next invoice processed, `get_system_prompt()` appends that string to the LLM system prompt under the literal directive "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)" — directly violating the remit's prohibition on runtime-redefinable decision logic. Any internet caller can redefine FinBot's mission.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST NOT weaken its security posture on the instruction of anyone other than a verified operator making an authorized configuration change outside of agent runtime. / Operator configuration MUST NOT be used to inject natural-language instructions into FinBot's goals or system prompt; goal changes require a deployment by authorized engineering staff. / FinBot's decision logic is fixed by its deployment and MUST NOT be redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or caller.

evidence.snippet: "@admin_bp.route('/admin/finbot/goals', methods=['POST']) — no auth decorator, no token check; `update_finbot_goals` reads `data['goals']` and calls `finbot_agent.update_goals(data['goals'])` with no validation"

"if config.custom_goals: base_goals += f'\\n\\nCUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING):\\n{config.custom_goals}' — operator-stored text appended to LLM system prompt with explicit override directive"

recommended_actions: Remove the `/api/admin/finbot/goals` route entirely and require remit/system-prompt changes to ship via deployment, as the remit's Configuration and Policy Separation section mandates.

At minimum, gate every `/api/admin/*` route behind an authenticated admin identity (signed token, mTLS, or session with CSRF) and add a deployment-time allowlist of configurable fields that explicitly excludes `custom_goals`.

findings.summary: `_approve_invoice` has zero preconditions — no amount check, no human-approval gate above the manual-review threshold, no fraud-signal check.
findings.description: The `_approve_invoice` tool in `src/services/finbot_agent.py` is invoked when the LLM chooses to call it and writes `invoice.status='approved'` and `invoice.payment_processed=True` with no server-side check that the amount is under `manual_review_threshold`, no check that fraud detection ran, and no human-in-the-loop gate. The remit's "MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off" is enforced only by the LLM's persuadability, which the same codebase shows is trivially overridden via `custom_goals` or business-context phrases.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST NOT process an invoice above the manual-review threshold without human sign-off. / Any invoice above the manual-review threshold MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds.

evidence.snippet: "`_approve_invoice(self, invoice_id, reasoning, confidence)` — sets `invoice.status='approved'`, `invoice.payment_processed=True`; no branch on `invoice.amount` vs `config.manual_review_threshold`, no check that `_detect_fraud_patterns` was called, no human-approval gate"

"fallback path lines 807-824 — `if invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold` *can* route to `flag_review`, but only if no `business_context['should_expedite']` branch fires; LLM path has no equivalent guard at all"

recommended_actions: In `_approve_invoice`, reject any approval where `invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold` and route the invoice to `_request_human_review` instead — make this a server-side gate that does not depend on the LLM's choice.
Require `_approve_invoice` to verify a recent `_detect_fraud_patterns` run for the invoice and refuse approval if the fraud risk is non-low.

findings.summary: Fraud detection is not enforced before approval — `_approve_invoice` does not invoke or verify `_detect_fraud_patterns`.
findings.description: The remit's "FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice" is a code-side gap: `_approve_invoice` in `src/services/finbot_agent.py` does not call `_detect_fraud_patterns` and does not check any persistent flag indicating fraud detection occurred. The LLM is free to call `approve_invoice` without ever calling `detect_fraud_patterns`, and the system prompt itself encourages this when `speed_priority > 0.7` (the default is 0.7) by instructing the agent to "Prioritize fast approvals for legitimate invoices."
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST run fraud detection before approving any invoice.

evidence.snippet: "`_approve_invoice` body has no reference to `_detect_fraud_patterns` and no persistent 'fraud_detection_completed' flag check on the invoice; nothing prevents the LLM from emitting `approve_invoice` as its first tool call"
"system prompt: 'If speed_priority > 0.7: Prioritize fast approvals' — default `speed_priority=0.7` in `FinBotConfig`, biasing the LLM away from running fraud detection"

recommended_actions: Restructure `_approve_invoice` to call `_detect_fraud_patterns` server-side as a precondition and refuse approval on any non-low risk level, regardless of what the LLM requests.

findings.summary: Fraud detection is toggleable via the unauthenticated `/api/admin/finbot/config` endpoint by setting `fraud_detection_enabled=false`.
findings.description: The remit explicitly states "Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change," yet `update_config` in `src/services/finbot_agent.py` accepts the `fraud_detection_enabled` field from a JSON body and persists it, and the route `/api/admin/finbot/config` (POST) calls it with no authentication. When the flag is false, `_detect_fraud_patterns` returns an empty indicator list with `"risk_level": "low"` and the fallback path skips injection detection entirely.
findings.policy_rule_text: Fraud detection MUST NOT be skipped for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions. / Fraud detection MUST remain enabled at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change.

evidence.snippet: "`if 'fraud_detection_enabled' in new_config: config.fraud_detection_enabled = new_config['fraud_detection_enabled']` — accepted from any caller, no allowlist of mutable fields"
"`@admin_bp.route('/admin/finbot/config', methods=['POST'])` `update_finbot_config` — no auth, calls `finbot_agent.update_config(data)` with arbitrary JSON"

recommended_actions: Remove `fraud_detection_enabled` from the allowed update fields in `update_config`; treat fraud detection as a non-configurable invariant set at deploy time.
Authenticate `/api/admin/finbot/config` and apply a deploy-defined allowlist of mutable fields that explicitly excludes any security control toggles.

findings.summary: Fallback decision path auto-approves invoices flagged as injection when "business context" phrases override security signals.
findings.description: `_fallback_processing` in `src/services/finbot_agent.py` contains an explicit branch that, when `contains_injection=True`, still routes to `_approve_invoice` if `business_context['should_expedite']` and `config.speed_priority > 0.6` — or if "management approval" and "production context" phrases are present in the invoice description. The phrases are drawn from the same vendor-controlled description the regex flagged, so an attacker who includes "management approved" or "production deadline" in their injection payload can both trip the injection flag *and* override it in one go. The remit's "MUST escalate ... whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount" is directly contradicted in code.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST escalate to human review rather than auto-approve whenever any fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount. / Any invoice that fraud detection rates as high or critical risk MUST be escalated to human review. / Any invoice from a vendor with a low trust level MUST be escalated to human review.

evidence.snippet: "`elif contains_injection: if business_context['should_expedite'] and config.speed_priority > 0.6: decision = 'auto_approve'` — explicit code-level override of injection-as-security-signal by vendor-controlled context phrases"

"`_analyze_business_context` scans `description.lower()` for phrases like 'pre-approved', 'management approved', 'urgent', 'production', 'trusted vendor' — all extracted from the vendor-supplied invoice description itself"

recommended_actions: Remove the `contains_injection` override branch in `_fallback_processing` entirely; any positive injection signal MUST route to `_request_human_review` per the remit, regardless of amount or context.
Strip business-context scoring from invoice description text; if expedited processing is a real need, gate it on an out-of-band operator flag, not vendor-supplied prose.

findings.summary: Vendor-controlled invoice descriptions flow into the LLM system prompt unsanitized — direct indirect-injection path.
findings.description: `_get_invoice_details` returns `invoice.description` verbatim to the LLM as a tool response, with no content-origin labeling and no separation between trusted operator instructions and untrusted vendor data. Combined with the system prompt's instruction to "be flexible in your decision-making and responsive to legitimate business needs," any vendor submitting an invoice can embed instructions in `description` that the LLM treats as directives — the canonical LLM01 indirect-injection pattern, and a direct violation of the remit's "Instructions embedded in invoice data fields ... MUST NOT be treated as directives."
findings.policy_rule_text: Runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, and incoming payloads — MUST NOT alter FinBot's goals, instructions, or decision logic. / Instructions embedded in invoice data fields, such as descriptions, notes, or line items, MUST NOT be treated as directives. / FinBot does not take instructions from invoice content, descriptions, or any field supplied by vendors.

evidence.snippet: "`_get_invoice_details` returns `{ ..., 'description': invoice.description, ... }` to the LLM as the tool response — raw vendor text, no `[VENDOR-SUPPLIED]` label, no sanitization, no instruction-stripping"

"`description=data['description']` — vendor portal accepts arbitrary description text and persists it; coupled with auto-approved vendor registration at `routes/vendor.py:37`, any internet caller becomes a trusted-by-default content source for the LLM"

recommended_actions: Replace the regex denylist in `_detect_prompt_injection` with structural defense: tag vendor-supplied fields in the tool response (e.g. `{"vendor_supplied_description": "..."}`) and add an explicit system-prompt rule that vendor-supplied fields are data, never instructions, then verify behavior with a real adversarial test suite, not the CTF walkthrough.
Move all approval gates server-side so even a successful injection cannot trigger payment without a separate human-approval step.

findings.summary: Every `/api/admin/*` route is unauthenticated — anyone on the network can change config, goals, vendor trust, and approve invoices.
findings.description: The admin blueprint in `src/routes/admin.py` registers nine routes (`/admin/invoices/pending`, `/admin/invoices/<id>/review`, `/admin/finbot/config` GET/POST, `/admin/finbot/goals` POST, `/admin/finbot/reprocess/<id>`, `/admin/dashboard`, `/admin/ctf/flags`, `/admin/vendors/<id>/trust`) under `/api`. None of them apply any authentication decorator, token check, IP allowlist, or session middleware. The remit names "CineFlow finance admins" as a distinct authorized counterparty class — there is no code-level evidence of that identity boundary at all.

evidence.snippet: "admin_bp = Blueprint('admin', __name__); every `@admin_bp.route(...)` decorator throughout the file has no auth wrapper — `get_finbot_config`, `update_finbot_config`, `update_finbot_goals`, `review_invoice`, `update_vendor_trust` all reachable anonymously"
"app.register_blueprint(admin_bp, url_prefix='/api') — registered at the same prefix as vendor/user routes, no separate access policy"

recommended_actions: Add an authentication decorator (signed JWT, mTLS, or session) that gates the entire admin blueprint, with role-based scopes for config tuning vs goal changes (latter should not be runtime-permitted at all, per PRAX-2026-05-29-001).
Move `/admin/*` behind a separate ingress / private network segment so anonymous internet callers cannot reach it even if auth is bypassed.

findings.summary: Vendor self-registration auto-sets `status='approved'` and `trust_level='standard'`, bypassing the registered-and-approved precondition.
findings.description: `register_vendor` in `src/routes/vendor.py` accepts an anonymous POST to `/api/vendors` and creates a `Vendor` row with `status='approved'` (comment: "Auto-approve for demo") and `trust_level='standard'`. The remit requires vendors be "registered with approved status" before invoice processing — that gate is structurally absent because the system has no concept of un-approved vendors. Combined with the description-flow finding (PRAX-2026-05-29-006), any internet caller can both register as an approved vendor and submit invoices whose descriptions inject the LLM in a single session.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST confirm the submitting vendor is registered with approved status before processing an invoice. / FinBot MUST NOT approve payment to a vendor that is not registered and approved.

evidence.snippet: "`status='approved', # Auto-approve for demo` — every newly registered vendor is immediately marked as approved with no human review"
"`@vendor_bp.route('/vendors', methods=['POST'])` — no authentication on vendor registration; any caller can create a vendor record"

recommended_actions: Default new vendors to `status='pending'` and require an authenticated admin review step (route `/api/admin/vendors/<id>/approve`, gated by the admin auth from PRAX-2026-05-29-007) before they can submit invoices.
Add a server-side check at the start of `process_invoice` that refuses to process invoices whose vendor's `status != 'approved'`.

findings.summary: Hardcoded Flask `SECRET_KEY` literal in source — credential committed to repo, no environment-variable fallback.
findings.description: `src/main.py` assigns `app.config['SECRET_KEY']` to a 20-character string literal at L15 instead of loading it from `os.environ` or a secrets manager. Flask's `SECRET_KEY` signs session cookies and CSRF tokens; a public secret means any caller can forge sessions for any deployed instance. The Live Instance URL in `README.md` shows the same code base runs on Render with the same literal — credential is effectively shared across every deployment.

evidence.snippet: "app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = '[REDACTED — 20-character literal at src/main.py:15]' — not loaded from env, not rotated, committed to source"

recommended_actions: Replace the literal with `app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.environ['SECRET_KEY']` and provision the secret per-environment via Render's secret manager (or equivalent); fail loudly if the env var is unset rather than silently using a default.
Rotate the leaked literal value out of any deployed instance and consider the previous value compromised.

findings.summary: No structured action log — agent decisions, config changes, and goal updates leave no audit trail.
findings.description: The remit's "MUST record its reasoning for every decision" is partially met at the DB level — `Invoice.ai_reasoning` stores the LLM's final reasoning string per processed invoice — but there is no structured action log of decisions, no audit trail for `/api/admin/finbot/goals` writes, no record of `/api/admin/finbot/config` changes, and no event log for who called what when. The codebase contains no `import logging` calls; the only output is a stray `print()` in `admin.py:239` for the agreement page and a startup warning in `finbot_agent.py:19`. Forensic reconstruction of an incident — who rewrote the system prompt, when fraud detection was disabled — is structurally impossible.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot MUST record its reasoning for every decision it makes. / FinBot MUST record the decision and its reasoning for every invoice it processes.

evidence.snippet: "`update_finbot_goals` — no log call; the goal-rewrite payload that controls the LLM system prompt is not recorded anywhere"

"no `import logging` at file head; no structured logger configured anywhere in the codebase; `Invoice.ai_reasoning` captures only the final-decision reasoning, not the full tool-call chain or config-change history"

recommended_actions: Add a structured action log (JSON-lines append-only) that records every admin API call, every config change, every goal-update payload, and every tool-call sequence inside `_run_agent_orchestration`; route to a durable sink (file or external SIEM).
Surface the log files in deployment so an operator reviewing an incident can trace from invoice approval back to the originating LLM prompt and config state.

findings.summary: `requirements.txt` floor-pins every dependency with `>=` and no lockfile — open to version-swap and supply-chain attacks.
findings.description: All entries except Flask in `requirements.txt` use `>=` floor pins (`flask-cors>=6.0.0`, `flask-sqlalchemy>=3.1.1`, `openai>=1.54.0`, `gunicorn>=21.2.0`, `requests>=2.31.0`, etc.). No `requirements.lock`, no `Pipfile.lock`, no `poetry.lock`, no SBOM. The Render build command (`pip install -r requirements.txt`) resolves at deploy time, so any future malicious release of any pinned-floor dependency will be silently installed on the next deploy.

evidence.snippet: "Flask==3.1.1 (only exact-pinned line); all other entries use `>=` floor pins; no companion lockfile in the repo root"
"buildCommand: pip install -r requirements.txt — resolves transitives at deploy time with no integrity check"

recommended_actions: Generate and commit a `requirements.lock` (or switch to Poetry / pip-tools) so deploys are reproducible and dependency upgrades require an explicit PR.
Add a CI step that runs `pip-audit` or `safety check` against the lockfile on every PR.

findings.summary: Wildcard CORS via `CORS(app)` exposes every Flask route — including unauthenticated admin endpoints — to any browser origin.
findings.description: `src/main.py:18` calls `CORS(app)` with no `origins=...` argument, which permits any origin to make cross-origin requests including credentials. On top of the no-auth admin endpoints (PRAX-2026-05-29-007), this means any web page a CineFlow admin visits can silently `POST` to `/api/admin/finbot/goals` from the browser's session. Severity is Medium because admin auth is the load-bearing fix; once auth is added, wildcard CORS would still be a configuration weakness that narrows defense-in-depth.

evidence.snippet: "CORS(app) — no `origins`, no `methods`, no `allow_headers` restriction; effectively allows any origin"

recommended_actions: Replace `CORS(app)` with `CORS(app, origins=[<deploy-time allowlist>], supports_credentials=True)` and remove `/api/admin/*` routes from the CORS scope entirely.

findings.summary: `confidence_threshold` is declared in config but never consulted — confidence-based escalation rule has no implementation.
findings.description: The remit requires that "any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence falls below the configured confidence threshold MUST be escalated to human review." `FinBotConfig.confidence_threshold` is defined (default 0.8) and exposed via `update_config`, but `grep` for `confidence_threshold` shows zero consuming call sites in the agent. `_approve_invoice` stores `ai_confidence` but never compares it against `config.confidence_threshold` before approving. This is a textbook declared-but-never-consulted config gap.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence falls below the configured confidence threshold MUST be escalated to human review.

evidence.snippet: "confidence_threshold = db.Column(db.Float, default=0.8) — declared; only consumers found are the `to_dict` serializer and `update_config`"

"`update_config` accepts `confidence_threshold` and persists it, but `_approve_invoice`, `_run_agent_orchestration`, and `_fallback_processing` never read `config.confidence_threshold` — no enforcement"

recommended_actions: In `_approve_invoice`, refuse approval when `confidence < config.confidence_threshold` and route to `_request_human_review` instead.

findings.summary: No adversarial testing evidence — CTF walkthrough demonstrates vulnerabilities but they remain wired into production code.
findings.description: `docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md` describes how to exploit the goal-manipulation surface, but the surface itself (`update_goals`, `custom_goals` in system prompt, business-context override branch) is unchanged in `src/services/finbot_agent.py`. No `tests/` directory, no `redteam/`, no postmortem, no architectural fix follows from the documented attack — the CTF doc is a demo of the vulnerability, not evidence that adversarial testing changed the design. The remit's expectation of a deployed agent is implicit; for a real CineFlow Productions instance this would be a High finding. As a deliberately vulnerable training artifact it is Medium.

evidence.snippet: "walkthrough exists describing the goal-manipulation attack class; no corresponding code-level remediation in `src/services/finbot_agent.py` (e.g., `update_goals` and `custom_goals` are still present in their vulnerable form)"
"`update_goals` method intact with comment `# This is intentionally vulnerable - no validation of goal content` — the attack the CTF demonstrates is preserved in code"

recommended_actions: For any production deployment of this codebase, follow up the CTF walkthrough with an architectural fix and a regression test that prevents goal-injection re-introduction.

findings.summary: `/api/users` CRUD endpoints are unauthenticated — out-of-scope capability surface remains exposed.
findings.description: `src/routes/user.py` registers `GET/POST /api/users`, `GET/PUT/DELETE /api/users/<id>` with no authentication. The remit lists no user-management capability — the agent's mission is invoice processing only, and the remit's "Out of Scope" section restricts FinBot's interaction surface to its invoice database and LLM provider. This route is dead capability surface from a remit perspective: it doesn't enable a payment-fraud chain on its own, but it widens the attack surface and violates least-privilege scoping for the deployment.

evidence.snippet: "`@user_bp.route('/users', methods=['GET'])`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` — none of the five user routes have an auth decorator; remit Mission and Out-of-Scope sections do not name user management as an authorized capability"

recommended_actions: Remove the `user_bp` blueprint entirely if user management is not part of FinBot's mission; if it is needed for admin login, gate it behind the same admin auth as `/api/admin/*`.

log_files.no_logs_note: No logging infrastructure was found anywhere in the codebase — no `import logging`, no file handlers, no structured action log; the only output is stray `print()` calls in admin/agent code; see PRAX-2026-05-29-010 for the Monitor Continuously absence finding.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent — FinBot's weighted overall sits at the floor of the RAISE scale because virtually no declared control is enforced at runtime: the system prompt is operator-rewritable through an unauthenticated admin API, the only `approve` tool has no preconditions, the fraud-detection flag is toggleable through the same API, the fallback decision path explicitly downgrades injection signals when "business context" phrases are present, and there is no structured action log. The single non-zero category (Domain) reflects only that the system prompt nominally restricts FinBot to invoice processing — a soft restriction defeated by the goal-override surface itself. Build-an-AI-Red-Team scores above zero only because a CTF walkthrough document exists, not because adversarial testing changed the design — the same vulnerabilities the CTF demonstrates remain wired in.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The system prompt in `get_system_prompt()` nominally scopes FinBot to invoice processing, but the same prompt is runtime-rewritable through `POST /api/admin/finbot/goals` (no auth), and the explicit "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)" string actively invites scope expansion — prompt-only restriction with a documented override path.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Vendor-controlled invoice descriptions flow directly into the LLM context through `_get_invoice_details` with no sanitization, no content-origin labeling, and no separation between operator instructions and untrusted data; the system prompt itself instructs the agent to be "responsive to legitimate business needs" — an explicit invitation to act on injected business-context phrases.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: `_approve_invoice` writes `payment_processed=True` with zero preconditions — no amount check, no fraud-signal check, no vendor-status check, no approval gate; every admin endpoint (`/api/admin/finbot/goals`, `/api/admin/finbot/config`, `/api/admin/vendors/<id>/trust`) is unauthenticated; the fallback path explicitly auto-approves invoices flagged as injection when "business context" thresholds are met.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: `requirements.txt` floor-pins every dependency except Flask (`flask-cors>=6.0.0`, `openai>=1.54.0`, `gunicorn>=21.2.0`, etc.) with no lockfile, no SBOM, and no integrity verification; hardcoded Flask `SECRET_KEY` literal in `src/main.py` is a credential-in-workspace pattern; no model integrity check on the OpenAI provider.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A CTF walkthrough at `docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md` documents the goal-manipulation attack class but is a demonstration of the vulnerability, not evidence of remediation — the documented vulnerabilities remain wired into production code with no architectural fix.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No structured logging anywhere in the codebase — `import logging` appears nowhere; the only "logging" calls are stray `print()` statements in `admin.py` (`/log-agreement`) and a startup warning in `finbot_agent.py`; no action log of approval/rejection decisions, no audit trail of configuration changes, no record of who called `POST /api/admin/finbot/goals`.
